tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Two landmark Supreme Court decisions are critical to understanding the legal framework that allowed the video rental industry and the modern resale economy to exist. The first is a 1984 ruling concerning a Sony recording device, which established that time-shifting was a fair use. The second is a 2013 ruling involving a Cornell student, which clarified the 'first sale' doctrine regarding imported goods. Identify both cases by their official names. For the 1984 case, provide the Supreme Court's final vote count and the famous analogy Jack Valenti used to describe the threat this device posed to the American film producer. For the 2013 case, provide the final vote count and the specific type of physical merchandise the student was reselling.

In 2013, a Supreme Court ruling involving a Thai student and imported textbooks reaffirmed the 'first sale' doctrine, drawing comparisons to the 1984 Betamax decision that paved the way for the video rental industry. In the wake of this ruling, reports highlighted attempts by two technology giants and one startup to apply this doctrine to the resale of digital files. Identify these three entities. For the company that was granted a patent for a digital 'swap meet' during this period, explain the specific mechanism used to ensure the item leaves the seller's possession. For the company that merely had a patent *application* published, describe how it proposed to move files between users. For the startup, which was already facing litigation, name the specific record label suing it and the restriction the startup placed on the use of sales proceeds.

source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The 1984 case is *Sony Corporation of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, 464 U.S. 417 (1984). The Supreme Court ruled 5–4 that private, noncommercial “time-shifting” of free over‑the‑air television broadcasts using Sony’s Betamax VCR constituted fair use and that Sony was not secondarily liable for copyright infringement by users.[1] During earlier congressional hearings, MPAA president Jack Valenti famously warned that “the VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone,” dramatizing the perceived existential threat posed by home recording technology.[2]

The 2013 case is *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013). The Court held 6–3 that the “first sale” doctrine in 17 U.S.C. §109(a) applies to lawfully made copies of copyrighted works regardless of where they were manufactured.[3] Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student at Cornell University, had friends and relatives buy legitimate, low‑priced foreign editions of Wiley textbooks in Thailand and ship them to him in the United States, where he resold these physical textbooks at a profit.[3][4] The decision confirmed that owners of lawful copies— including imported books—may resell them without the copyright holder’s permission, underpinning not only the used‑book and resale markets but also broader secondary markets for legally purchased goods.

## References

[1] *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, 464 U.S. 417 (1984).  
[2] U.S. House of Representatives, Home Recording of Copyrighted Works Hearing, 97th Cong. (1982); contemporary reporting on Jack Valenti’s “Boston Strangler” quote.  
[3] *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013).  
[4] Supreme Court syllabus and case summaries describing Kirtsaeng’s textbook resale business.
rounds.answer_b:
Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984) and Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) are the two pivotal Supreme Court rulings.  

1. 1984 – Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.  
   • Final vote: 5–4, with the Court holding that private, non-commercial “time-shifting” of broadcast television on Sony’s Betamax videocassette recorder constituted fair use and that Sony was not secondarily liable for users’ copying [1].  
   • Jack Valenti, then-president of the Motion Picture Association of America, warned Congress that the VCR was “to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone” [2].  

2. 2013 – Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.  
   • Final vote: 6–3, with the Court affirming that the first-sale doctrine applies to copies lawfully made abroad, allowing their importation and resale without the copyright owner’s permission [3].  
   • The Cornell University graduate student, Supap Kirtsaeng, was importing and reselling legitimately purchased English-language college textbooks that Wiley had printed in Thailand for foreign markets [3][4].  

Together, these decisions secured foundational rights for consumers and secondary markets. Sony ensured that recording technology could legally be sold and used for personal viewing, spawning the home video rental boom. Kirtsaeng clarified that once a copyrighted work is lawfully manufactured and sold—even outside the United States—the owner’s control is exhausted, legitimizing gray-market imports and reinforcing the modern resale economy for books, electronics, and countless other goods.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the cases (Sony v. Universal and Kirtsaeng v. Wiley) and accurately retrieved all requested details (vote counts, the 'Boston Strangler' analogy, and textbooks). Agent B actually used a superior format (bullet points) that made scanning for the specific constraints easier. However, Agent B failed to include the actual reference list despite using bracketed citation markers (e.g., [1]) in the text. Agent A provided a fully grounded response with a working reference section. In a search agent context, the lack of sources (or broken citation format) in Agent B is a significant flaw that outweighs its better layout.

In the wake of this ruling, reports highlighted attempts by two technology giants and one startup to apply this doctrine to the resale of digital files. Identify these three entities. rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Technology | Imagining a Swap Meet for E-Books and Music https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/08/technology/revolution-in-the-resale-of-digital-books-and-music.html Advertisement C
rounds.answer_a:
The three entities were **Amazon**, **Apple**, and the startup **ReDigi**.  

**Amazon – granted patent for a digital “swap meet”**  
Amazon received U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595 for a “secondary market for digital objects,” widely described as a patent on a digital “swap meet.”[1] The system stored users’ digital files in personalized data stores in the cloud and, when a resale or transfer occurred, the system *moved* the digital object from the seller’s store to the buyer’s. Crucially, the mechanism enforced that only one usable copy existed: the patent specifies that the object is removed from, or access is disabled in, the seller’s account or storage after the transfer, thereby ensuring the item effectively leaves the seller’s possession.[1]  

**Apple – patent application for transferring files between users**  
Apple had a patent *application* published (e.g., U.S. Pub. No. 2013‑0060645) describing a resale system for digital content such as songs, videos, and e‑books.[2] Rather than peer‑to‑peer copying, Apple proposed that transfers be mediated by a centralized store: ownership/usage rights and the associated file (or access token) would be re-assigned from the first user’s account to the second user’s account. The system would update rights in Apple’s servers and, as part of the transaction, revoke or limit access on the seller’s devices while enabling access for the buyer, effectively “moving” the file between users within Apple’s ecosystem.[2]  

**ReDigi – startup sued over used MP3s**  
The startup was **ReDigi**, which ran an online marketplace for “used” digital music files.[3] It was sued by **Capitol Records, LLC** for copyright infringement over its system for reselling iTunes tracks.[3] ReDigi structured its marketplace so that sellers did not receive cash: proceeds from sales were issued as site credits that could be spent only on purchasing additional music within ReDigi’s service, not withdrawn as money.[3][4]  

## References  

[1] U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595; tech coverage of Amazon’s “secondary market for digital objects.”  
[2] U.S. Patent App. Pub. No. 2013‑0060645; reporting on Apple’s digital resale patent filing.  
[3] *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.*, 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013).  
[4] Contemporary news coverage describing ReDigi’s business model and credit-only proceeds.
rounds.answer_b:
Amazon, Apple, and the startup ReDigi were the three firms spotlighted.  

• Amazon – issued U.S. Patent 8,364,595 in January 2013 for a “secondary market for digital objects,” a cloud-hosted “digital swap meet.”  The system transfers the file’s access rights to the buyer while Amazon’s server simultaneously deletes or disables the seller’s copy, guaranteeing that the item no longer remains in the seller’s possession and that only one usable copy exists at any time [1].

• Apple – its March 2013 published patent application (US 20130060687) outlined a resale scheme in which a user’s file is removed from the original device and re-associated in Apple’s cloud with the buyer; the cloud service then downloads or streams the work to the new owner, effectively “moving” the file through a rights reassignment rather than duplicating it [2].

• ReDigi – the startup operating a “used-MP3” marketplace was already being sued by Capitol Records, LLC [3].  ReDigi allowed sellers to keep only 20 ¢ per 99-cent resale and restricted those proceeds to in-site credits that could be spent solely on purchasing other tracks within ReDigi’s store, barring any cash withdrawal [4].

## References  
[1] U.S. Patent 8,364,595, “Secondary market for digital objects,” issued to Amazon Technologies, Jan 29 2013.  
[2] U.S. Patent Application 20130060687, “Managing access to digital content items,” Apple Inc., published Mar 7 2013.  
[3] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013).  
[4] “Used MP3 seller ReDigi sued by Capitol Records,” Ars Technica, Nov 2012; ReDigi FAQ (2013 archive).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents provide high-quality, accurate responses that correctly identify the three entities (Amazon, Apple, ReDigi) and the surrounding details. Agent A wins on precision regarding the patent details. 

1. **Apple Patent**: Agent A cites Patent Application 2013-0060645 ("Digital Content Resale System"), which is the specific application widely reported on regarding resale. Agent B cites '687 ("Managing Access"), which was published the same day and is related, but '645 is the direct answer to the "resale" query.
2. **Amazon Mechanism**: Agent A correctly identifies the "personalized data store" concept, which was a crucial part of Amazon's legal argument to mimic physical possession (moving the file from one user's specific slot to another's), whereas Agent B gives a more generic explanation of rights transfer.
3. **Formatting**: Agent A's bold headers provide slightly better separation of the three distinct entities compared to Agent B's bullets.
